content.full_text:
Placer County Counsel
175 FULWEILER AVENUE, ROOM 301 – COUNTY ADMINISTRATIVE CENTER
AUBURN, CALIFORNIA 95603-4581 • (916) 889-4044

March 1, 1990

Cathy Donovan
Fair Political Practices
Commission, Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request For Advice, Conflict of Interest Issue

Dear Cathy:

As the County Counsel for Placer County, it is my responsibility to advise County government on procedures to be followed in the adoption of general plan documentes. An issue has arisen in our County regarding the participation in general plan hearings of public officials who own stock in a local telephone company, whose service area includes territory within the ambit of one of the general plans under consideration. You may recall that we discussed this issue in your office in 1989. Our office requests written assistance on this issue.

ISSUE:

May two members of the Placer County Planning Commission, and one elected number of the Board of Supervisors of Placer County, participate and vote in public hearings on the adoption of the Dry Creek West Placer Community Plan, if they each own stock in the Roseville Telephone Company, which provides service to areas covered by the proposed plan?

INFORMATION PROVIDED:

For the benefit of your staff, we are providing the following documents on this issue:

1. A current draft of the proposal community plan.

2. The most recently published annual report of the Roseville Telephone Company.

3. Current copies of the Form 721 filed by each public official in question, supplied by our local filing officer.

We also enclose correspondence from a local community
group, questioning the possibility of a conflict of interest.

COMMENTS:

We offer the following comments as an opinion on the
question submitted.

I. THE COMMUNITY PLAN IS A GENERAL PLANNING DOCUMENT WITH
NO DIRECT FINANCIAL EFFECT ON ANY SPECIFIC PROJECT OR
PROPOSAL.

Placer County first adopted a County-wide General Plan in
1967. Since that time, the diversity of the County led to the
development of 20 localized Community Plans. These documents are
prepared by planning professionals and consultants, and then are
the subject of a series of workshops and public hearings, as well
as environmental review, to obtain public input into the planning
process.

This plan is "the official statement of the County of
Placer setting forth goals, policies, and implementation
proposals that will guide development in the area for at least the
year 2000." The plan must be consistent with the County-wide
General Plan, and is simply more geographically specific. (See
DRAFT DRY CREEK WEST PLACER COMMUNITY PLAN, pages 1 - 8.)

Landowners frequently attempt to develop property that is
not classified for development in a general plan or community
plan. They are then required to apply for a general plan
amendment, for the specific real property they wish to develop.
Such action is quite distinct from the general plan process, and
may indeed result in a direct financial effect.

The City of Roseville had similar difficulties with
ownership of Roseville Telephone Company stock in 1988, however,
the planning activities under consideration there involved
specific development proposals for specific parcels of land with
well defined customer potential for the telephone company. That
process raised clearly a different issue than the Placer County
program of general plan development. For the City the process
created a clear nexus between the proposed development projects
and the direct financial effect on the telephone company. For
the County, the issue is simply the general political concept of
growth.

This distinction is most eloquently stated in the January
11, 1990, letter (enclosed) from the West Placer Citizens'


Committee, where that group attempts to paraphrase the FPPC
advice to the City of Roseville:

"It has also come to our attention that the Fair
Political Practices Commission ruled that city
and county officials owning more than $1,000 in
telephone company stock were ineligible to vote
on major growth issues." (Paragraph 4, final
sentence.)

We believe this is a reference to the October 6, 1988,
advice letter No. I-88-316 to Michael F. Dean, City Attorney of
Roseville. The vagueness of the discussion in that letter led to
a dialogue on the possibility of clarifying the regulations.
Some effort was made to modify Section 18703 regarding the
"effect on the Public Generally" provisions of your regulations.
It is apparent that a point of clarity has still not been reached
in defining the gap between specific development proposals and
general growth policies for purposes of determining conflicts of
interest.

III. THE GENERAL ISSUE OF GROWTH POLICY MUST BE DISTINGUISHED
FROM CONFLICTS RELATED TO FINANCIAL INTERESTS IN SPECIFIC
DEVELOPMENT PROPOSALS.

The argument for attempting to find a conflict of interest
in a general growth policy is set forth again in the letter
enclosed, in paragraph 5:

"If extensive growth in the area is allowed,
company stockholders will benefit financially
by the creation of the increased customers."

It is not difficult to see how this same reasoning could
be applied to virtually any business within and near a planning
area. Since growth of an area will foreseeably increase the
customer base of any local business, this reasoning process would
presume to exclude anyone who profits from a local business
enterprise from participating in local government planning
activities. It would effectively disenfranchise a major segment
of our population from public office.

FPPC regulations focus on material and specific financial
effects of the decision making process. We have reviewed Section
18702 and 18702.1 with respect to materiality. While it is clear
that each individual's financial interest in Roseville Telephone
must be examined for financial significance at specified
thresholds, the regulations also speak to the direct


I cannot provide an accurate verbatim transcription of this document as the text is too faint or degraded in the provided image for me to read it reliably.

involvement of the business entity in the decision before the
agency:

"Any business entity . . .in which the official has
a direct or indirect investment . . . is directly
involved in a decision before the official's
agency".  18702.1(2).

Where the governmental decision before the agency does not
involve a grant of specific development rights, permit or
license to any project, individual or business entity, the mere
fact that one possible outcome of the decision process may be
general growth of the area is certainly not a material financial
effect within this definition.  There have been no allegations of
real property ownership by Roseville Telephone in this project
are, nor any proposal for development by the company.

Even assuming that financial benefits may foreseeably flow
from general growth of an area to the local business community
and property owners, that does not give rise to a policy of
exclusion of all such beneficiaries from participation in the
political process.  Nor can such an argument be limited to
beneficial effects.  The regulations are clearly worded to
include effects that may be detrimental to the financial
interests of participants.  Thus if anti-growth advocates are
motivated by concern for the effect of general growth policies on
property values within and adjacent to the planning area, they
also would be excluded from participation in the political
process by virtue of land ownership.  One must wonder who
be left to represent the area in shaping its growth future.


III.  COMMUNITY PLANS AND OTHER GENERAL PLAN DOCUMENTS EFFECT
THE PUBLIC GENERALLY.

Our professional planners have indicated to us that
although the planning process has been divided into local
community plans, none of them stand independent of the
County-wide General Plan, of which they are essentially
components.  In practical effect, each community plan component
affects the whole, and all portions of the county are impacted by
general planning policies adopted for each community of
County.  For example, the development of industrial resources in
one plan area of the County will impact on housing planning for
many other plan areas.

Section 18703 sets forth a regulation calling for
examination of the effect of undefined types of governmental
decisions on the interests of the public or official participant.
It does allow that common effects may be felt by "a significant


I apologize, but I cannot provide an accurate transcription of this document as the text is too faint or unclear to read reliably.

segment of the public," but also excludes an industry, trade or
profession unless the official is appointed specifically by a
legislative scheme to represent that group. In our examination
of Form 721 as submitted by the members of our Planning
Commission and the elected members of the Board of Supervisors,
we find a wide variety of interests in businesses, including:
real estate developers, medical/dental professional offices,
liquor stores, local banks, agricultural operations, title
companies, local savings and loan companies, etc.

     While all officials are advised to disclose and withdraw
from participation in permits and project approvals involving
financial interests and transactions to which they may be
personally a party, there is no blanket prohibition against any
class or profession from being actively involved in general
policymaking functions of their elected or appointed office.
(See 64 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 795). We likewise can see no
distinction for prohibiting persons who own businesses or stock
in local businesses from participating in such general planning
activity, so long as they scrupulously abstain from decisions
involving specific projects or applications affecting their own
financial interests.

REQUEST:

     We understand that this request may be approaching a more
substantive and less ministerial application of your regulations.
We are, however, faced with hearings in mid to late April, 1990,
on this Community Plan. It would be appreciated if you could
provide written advice on your position prior to April 13 if
possible. We have attempted to provide as complete a package and
interpretative position as possible to assist you. We will be
happy to meet with you or discuss any questions by telephone.

     Thank you for your consideration of this matter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        RICHARD K. DENHALTER,
                                        County Counsel

[illegible]

PLACER COUNTY
JAN 1 2 1990

Director Heidi Tschudin
Community Development Dept.
Planning Division
11414 B Ave
Auburn, CA 95603

PLANNING DIVISION

[RECEIVED STAMP: 11 JAN 1990]

The West Placer Citizens' Committee (WPCC) was formed in 1987 to unify and
represent the citizens of the Dry Creek - West Placer Community Plan area and
to function as an advocate on issues impacting anyone within the above area.
Participation and involvement in the WPCC has been and will continue to be
open and on a voluntary basis. As with any citizens' group, not every citizen
will choose to participate or to be represented by the group. By virtue of
WPCC questionnaires and results obtained from respondents, however, it
appears that the WPCC does in fact represent the majority for the area.

During the Placer County Planning Commission hearing of 13 December 1989 on
the Dry Creek - West Placer Community Plan, it became apparent that Planning
Commissioner, Jack Lish, was inclined to dispute the authenticity of the WPCC
formed within his area of responsibility. Since its formation, Mr Lish has
attended meetings conducted by the WPCC on major issues impacting the area.
Mr Lish's actions on 13 December 1989 elucidate the fact that Mr Lish is not
nor probably has not been in touch with the special problems and concerns of
his own constituency.

It has come to our attention that state laws prohibit government officials
from taking part in decisions affecting their finances, property or family
members. It has also come to our attention that the state Fair Political
Practices Commission ruled that city or county officials owning more than
$1,000 in telephone company stock were ineligible to vote on major growth
issues.

Our research of Placer County Election Division records indicates Mr Lish does
in fact own between $1,000 and $10,000 of common stock in the Roseville
Telephone Company (Form 721, "Statement of Economic Interest" dated 8 February
1989). In addition to Planning Commissioner Lish, Supervisor Robert Mahan
owns Roseville Telephone Company common stock valued at over $100,000
receiving a return on investment of over $10,000 (per Form 721, "Statement of
Economic Interest" dated 22 February 1989) and Mr Ross Riolo, Director,
District 1 of the Placer County Water Agency receives income of between $1,000
and $10,000 from the Placer County Water Agency (per Form 730, "Statement of
Economic Interest for Designated Employees" dated 24 February 1989).

If extensive growth in the area is allowed, company stockholders will benefit
financially by the creation of increased customers. As a result these
three gentlemen, who currently hold decision making positions, could
financially benefit by pro growth, high density development decision.

In view of the foregoing, we hereby request that an investigation be performed
relative to a potential conflict of interest involving the aforementioned
gentlemen and that they also be dismissed from any hearings or involvement in
any decision making process as it may relate to the Dry Creek - West Placer
Community Plan.


Respectfully,

Noe O. Fierros
NOE O. FIERROS
Core Committee Member
West Placer Citizens' Committee
(916) 771-0689

cooponents. In practical effect, each conmunity plan conponent
affects the whole, and all portions of the County are impicted by
general planning policies adopted for each connunity of the
county. For exanple, the development of industrial resources in
one plan area of the County will inpact on housing planning for
many other plan areas.
Section 18703 sets forth a regutation calling for
exanination of the effect of undefined types of gov6rnrnental
decisions on the interests of the puutic-6rri"iai paiiicipi"t.
It does allow that connon effects rnay be felt by ri signiii-ant


segrment of the public,rr but also excludes an industry, trade or
profession unless the official is appointed specifically by a
Iegislative scheme to represent that group. In our examination
of Form 72L as subnj.tted by the nenobers of our Planning
Cornmission and the elected menbers of the Board of Supervisors,
rre find a wide variety of interests in businesses, including:
real estate developers, nedical/dental professj-onal offices,
liquor stores, local banks, agriculturaL operations, title
cornpanies, loca} savings and loan companies, etc.
While all officials are advised to disclose and withdra!,
fron participation in pernits and project approvals involving
financiaL interests and transactj-ons to which they may be
personally a party, there is no blanket prohibition against any
class or profession fron being actively involved in general
policymaking functions of their elected or appointed office.
(see 64 ops.cal.Atty.Gen. 795). we likewise can see no
distinction for prohibiting persons who own businesses or stock
in loca1 businesses fron participating in such general planning
activity, so long as they scrupulously abstain from decisions
involving specific projects or applications affecting their own
financial interests.
REOUEST:
we understand that this request rnay be approaching a more
substantive and less rninisterial application of your reg'ulations.
we are, hovirever, faced with hearinqs j.n nrid to late April, 1990 ,
on this conmunity Plan. It hrould be appreciated if you could
provide written advice on your position prior to April 13 if
possible. we have attenpted to provide as conplete a package and
interpretative position as possible to assist you. We r^rill" be
happy to meet r^rith you or discuss any questions by telephone.
Thank you for your consideration of this matter.
embedding.first_500_words: Placer County Counsel 175 FULWEILER AVENUE, ROOM 301 – COUNTY ADMINISTRATIVE CENTER AUBURN, CALIFORNIA 95603-4581 • (916) 889-4044 March 1, 1990 Cathy Donovan Fair Political Practices Commission, Legal Division 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Request For Advice, Conflict of Interest Issue Dear Cathy: As the County Counsel for Placer County, it is my responsibility to advise County government on procedures to be followed in the adoption of general plan documentes. An issue has arisen in our County regarding the participation in general plan hearings of public officials who own stock in a local telephone company, whose service area includes territory within the ambit of one of the general plans under consideration. You may recall that we discussed this issue in your office in 1989. Our office requests written assistance on this issue. ISSUE: May two members of the Placer County Planning Commission, and one elected number of the Board of Supervisors of Placer County, participate and vote in public hearings on the adoption of the Dry Creek West Placer Community Plan, if they each own stock in the Roseville Telephone Company, which provides service to areas covered by the proposed plan? INFORMATION PROVIDED: For the benefit of your staff, we are providing the following documents on this issue: 1. A current draft of the proposal community plan. 2. The most recently published annual report of the Roseville Telephone Company. 3. Current copies of the Form 721 filed by each public official in question, supplied by our local filing officer. We also enclose correspondence from a local community group, questioning the possibility of a conflict of interest. COMMENTS: We offer the following comments as an opinion on the question submitted. I. THE COMMUNITY PLAN IS A GENERAL PLANNING DOCUMENT WITH NO DIRECT FINANCIAL EFFECT ON ANY SPECIFIC PROJECT OR PROPOSAL. Placer County first adopted a County-wide General Plan in 1967. Since that time, the diversity of the County led to the development of 20 localized Community Plans. These documents are prepared by planning professionals and consultants, and then are the subject of a series of workshops and public hearings, as well as environmental review, to obtain public input into the planning process. This plan is "the official statement of the County of Placer setting forth goals, policies, and implementation proposals that will guide development in the area for at least the year 2000." The plan must be consistent with the County-wide General Plan, and is simply more geographically specific. (See DRAFT DRY CREEK WEST PLACER COMMUNITY PLAN, pages 1 - 8.) Landowners frequently attempt to develop property that is not classified for development in a general plan or community plan. They are then required to apply for a general plan amendment, for the specific real property they wish to develop. Such action is quite distinct from the general plan process, and may indeed result in a direct financial effect. The City of Roseville had similar difficulties with ownership of Roseville Telephone Company stock in 1988, however, the
